competitors.label: Lifecycle Hooks
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue lifecycle guide and the strongest official baseline for hook timing.
theirStrengths: The official guide is authoritative on hook timing and how lifecycle callbacks are registered.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly shaped as an interview answer about choosing the right hook for a task.

realWorldUseCases: This page is narrower: when hooks fire and where side effects belong.
actionableExamples: Measure an element or start a DOM-only library

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain the lifecycle sequence clearly, but they are less focused on the interview question of safe placement for side effects.
actionableExamples: They remain strong on the lifecycle API surface, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The official page is still stronger on authoritative lifecycle timing, so the follow-up criterion remains close.
Add one explicit Composition API mapping note like mounted -> onMounted and unmounted -> onUnmounted.

competitors.winReason: The official guide is canonical on lifecycle timing, but our page now adds a task-to-hook matrix and a concrete mounted-versus-updated-versus-cleanup example that makes the answer more actionable in interview and debugging contexts.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because lifecycle timing and component behavior are core Vue fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a broad Vue interview-prep resource with strong fundamentals coverage.
theirGaps: The landing page is not a dedicated lifecycle comparison and is less focused on hook-placement follow-ups.

realWorldUseCases: Use them to place side effects safely: setup/init, DOM work, and cleanup at the right stage.
followUpConfusion: The strongest interview signal is explaining why a task belongs in one hook and not another.

realWorldUseCases: The question bank clearly covers Vue fundamentals, but it is less explicit about safe side-effect placement for this exact topic.
actionableExamples: Its solved-question model keeps examples competitive, so this criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less explicit about hook-selection reasoning than our dedicated answer.

actionableExamples: Add one shorter checklist mapping fetch, DOM access, and cleanup to mounted, updated, and unmounted.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is a strong broad interview resource, but our page is more focused on the exact hook-choice reasoning interviewers ask about next.

competitors.label: Vue Component Lifecycle Hooks
competitors.selectionReason: Vue School has a direct topic lesson on component lifecycle hooks and how to use them.
theirStrengths: It is a direct lifecycle-hooks lesson and therefore a strong topical tutorial baseline.
theirGaps: The surfaced lesson summary is lighter on interview framing and on why one hook is preferred over another for the same task.

followUpConfusion: If the interviewer asks "Why did Vue introduce setup(), ref(), and reactive()?" , switch to What are Vue Composition APIs (setup(), ref, reactive) and why were they introduced? .
actionableExamples: Read layout after a reactive update

realWorldUseCases: The lesson already explains when lifecycle hooks fire and how they are useful, so the use-case comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: It is a direct hooks lesson, so actionable teaching remains close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about scope boundaries and what question should move to the separate Composition API answer.

realWorldUseCases: Add a more concrete DOM-read versus DOM-write example tied to mounted and updated.

competitors.winReason: Vue School is a direct lifecycle lesson, but our page now turns the topic into a more reusable interview answer with a task-to-hook decision table and a concrete post-update measurement plus cleanup example.
